success_surface: Diagnosed root cause of #VALUE error (formulas returning text), reconstructed calculation at source data level instead of parsing text, used proper Excel formula syntax with TEXT function and string concatenation
effective_behaviors: Investigate actual cell contents before implementing fixes to understand if issues stem from data type mismatches or formula evaluation problems
When source cells contain formulas returning mixed text/numbers, trace back to underlying data tables and recreate calculations directly rather than attempting text extraction
Write complex Excel formulas as complete raw strings to preserve functionality rather than pre-evaluating values in Python
Use Excel TEXT() function with format codes for controlled number display, combined with & operator for appending text labels
Maintain file integrity by saving modifications to a new output path while keeping the original workbook untouched
evidence: Agent inspected B5-B8 cell values first, discovered they contained formulas like =SUMPRODUCT(...)&" TRADES" rather than pure numbers
Instead of attempting VALUE()/NUMBERVALUE() parsing on text-returning formulas, agent rewrote formula using COUNTIFS/COUNTIF directly on Trades sheet data
Formula written as '=TEXT(SUMPRODUCT(...)/SUMPRODUCT(...),"0.00%")&" WIN RATE"' preserved both percentage formatting and text suffix
Output saved to /mnt/data/outputs/.../1_49036_output.xlsx leaving source file untouched

memory_items.title: Diagnostic Cell Inspection
memory_items.lesson: Before implementing a fix, always inspect the actual cell contents (values, formulas, data types) to identify root causes of errors like #VALUE
memory_items.generalization: For any calculation error, examine the referenced cells programmatically to determine if the issue stems from data type mismatch, formula evaluation, or cell content structure before proceeding with solutions

memory_items.title: Source Data Reconstruction Pattern
memory_items.lesson: When target cells contain formulas that concatenate numbers with text, go back to the source data tables and recreate the calculation logic at the raw formula level
memory_items.generalization: Prefer recalculating from source data over parsing text outputs; this is more robust and avoids fragile string extraction operations that may break with data changes

memory_items.title: Excel Formula String Construction
memory_items.lesson: Complex formulas combining numerical calculations, formatting, and text labels should be written as complete raw strings preserving Excel's native functions
memory_items.generalization: Use TEXT(format_code) for number presentation control, & operator for text concatenation, and SUMPRODUCT/COUNTIF family for conditional aggregation—write entire expression as a single formula string

edits.content:
## 6. Error Diagnosis & Formula Strategy
- **Cell Content Inspection**: Before implementing fixes for calculation errors, always inspect actual cell contents programmatically. Determine if referenced cells contain formulas returning text vs. pure numbers.
- **Source Reconstruction**: When cells contain formulas that concatenate numbers with text (causing #VALUE on arithmetic), trace back to underlying data tables and recreate calculations at the raw formula level rather than attempting text parsing.
- **Formula Preservation**: Write complete Excel formulas as raw strings including all nested functions. Use TEXT("format_code") for controlled display formatting and & operator for appending text labels.
- **Robust Calculation Design**: Prefer direct reference to source data ranges over intermediate calculated cells when building dependent formulas; this reduces fragility and maintains calculation integrity across data updates.
